title: Gulab Jamun

subs.name: 3 tbsp full-fat milk powder worked into 1 tbsp ghee plus 1 tbsp cream — knead into a firm paste and use in place of khoya
subs.notes: fresh khoya gives the best texture

subs.name: 1 tbsp fine semolina (rava/sooji) for slightly chewier jamuns

ingredients.name: milk, at room temperature
visual_checks: add only if dough is too stiff
ingredients.notes: add only if dough is too stiff

subs.name: 2 drops kewra (pandanus) essence or simply omit for an unflavoured syrup

steps.text: Combine sugar, water and cardamom pods in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the sugar dissolves. Bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer uncovered for 6 minutes to form a thin, lightly sticky syrup — it should not reach one-thread consistency. Remove from heat, stir in rose water and keep warm on the lowest flame.

Grate or crumble the khoya into a wide bowl and knead it for 2 minutes until smooth and slightly warm from your hands. Add the maida, baking soda and ghee, then knead again for 1 minute to form a uniform, soft dough. It should hold a ball shape without cracking; add milk only by the drop if the dough feels too stiff.

Divide the dough into 18–20 equal portions (about 12 g each) and roll each between your palms into a smooth ball with no surface cracks — cracks let oil in and cause the jamuns to split. Work quickly so the dough doesn't dry out; cover the shaped balls with a damp cloth.

Pour oil into a wide kadai to a depth of at least 7 cm and heat over medium-low to about 140°C. Test with a small piece of dough — it should rise to the surface slowly after 3 seconds. Slide 5–6 balls into the oil and fry, swirling the pan or gently stirring, so they colour evenly on all sides. Fry for 10–12 minutes per batch until they are a deep mahogany-brown all over. Patience is essential; rushing the heat will burn the outside while the centre stays raw.

Remove the jamuns with a slotted spoon, shake off excess oil briefly and lower them straight into the warm sugar syrup. Repeat with remaining batches. Once all the jamuns are in the syrup, cover the pan and let them soak undisturbed for at least 1 hour at room temperature — they will swell and absorb syrup visibly. Serve warm, or refrigerate and gently rewarm before serving.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: khoya → 3 tbsp full-fat milk powder worked into 1 tbsp ghee plus 1 tbsp cream — knead into a firm paste and use in place of khoya (fresh khoya gives the best texture); maida → 1 tbsp fine semolina (rava/sooji) for slightly chewier jamuns; rose-water → 2 drops kewra (pandanus) essence or simply omit for an unflavoured syrup
